title: Prompt Ensembling
concept: Combining outputs from multiple differently-phrased prompts to reduce sensitivity to any single wording.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: In Best-of-N Sampling, how do you pick the final answer from N generated candidates?

options.text: You use a scoring function or reward model to select the best candidate
You always pick the very first candidate generated
You throw out all candidates and generate one more

exercises.explain: Best-of-N Sampling generates multiple candidate answers and then uses a scoring function or reward model to judge and select the strongest one — that's different from prompt ensembling, where instead of varying samples, you vary the prompt WORDING and combine results.

cards.title: Does Rewording Change the Answer?
cards.body: Ask an AI 'What's the capital of Australia?' and then 'Which city serves as Australia's capital?' Sometimes tiny wording changes nudge a model toward different or shakier answers. What if you asked it several different ways and combined the results?

cards.title: What Is Prompt Ensembling?
cards.body: Prompt ensembling means rephrasing the same question in several different ways, sending each version to the model, and then combining the answers (often by majority vote). This reduces the chance that one weirdly-worded prompt throws off your final result.

cards.title: Like Asking Several Friends the Same Question, Differently
cards.body: If you ask one friend 'What's 7x8?' they might mess up. But if you ask three friends who each phrase it their own way, and they all agree on 56, you trust that answer way more than any single friend's guess.

cards.title: Same Question, Three Phrasings
cards.body: Prompt 1: 'A store has 120 apples and sells 45. How many are left?' Prompt 2: 'If you start with 120 apples and remove 45, what remains?' Prompt 3: 'Compute 120 minus 45.' All three should say 75 — and if two or three agree, you can trust 75 much more than trusting just one phrasing.

cards.title: Myth: More Wording Variety Always Means More Randomness
cards.body: Actually it's the opposite! Varying the prompt wording and then combining answers (like voting) usually makes results MORE stable, not less. Each phrasing has its own tiny biases, but those biases tend to cancel out when you look for agreement across several of them.

cards.title: One Phrasing vs. Many Phrasings
cards.body: Relying on a single prompt is like betting everything on one opinion. Ensembling prompts is like polling a small group phrased differently, then trusting the majority view.

left.text: One wording, one shot — if that phrasing confuses the model, your only answer is the confused one.

right.text: Several wordings, then vote — if most phrasings agree, you get a far more reliable final answer.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of prompt ensembling?

options.text: To make the model respond faster
To reduce the risk that one particular prompt phrasing skews the final answer
To make prompts shorter and cheaper
To train a brand-new model from scratch

exercises.explain: Prompt ensembling exists to guard against wording sensitivity: since a model might answer slightly differently depending on how a question is phrased, combining multiple phrasings and looking for agreement gives a more trustworthy final answer.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Prompt ensembling requires using the exact same prompt text every time you query the model.
exercises.explain: False. Prompt ensembling specifically means using DIFFERENT phrasings of the same underlying question, not the same exact text repeated. That variety is what helps cancel out wording-specific quirks.

exercises.sentence: Prompt ensembling combines outputs from multiple differently-phrased prompts to reduce sensitivity to any single wording.
exercises.explain: The key word is 'differently-phrased' — the whole point is varying the wording of the prompt itself, not just repeating the exact same prompt multiple times.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three descriptions of 'prompt ensembling' is the AI-generated (least accurate/most off-base) one?

options.text: Rephrase your question multiple ways, send each to the model, and combine the answers to reduce reliance on any one wording.
Prompt ensembling means writing one perfect prompt so precisely worded that the model can never make a mistake.
Ask the same question in a few different styles, then go with the answer most versions agree on.

exercises.explain: Option B misunderstands the technique — prompt ensembling isn't about crafting one flawless prompt (that's prompt engineering for a single prompt). It's specifically about using MULTIPLE different phrasings and combining them, because no single wording can be guaranteed perfect.

exercises.prompt: You're building a math-tutor bot. Which setup best reduces the risk of the bot giving a wrong answer just because of how ONE prompt happened to be worded?

options.text: Write the single best prompt you can and always use it
Rewrite the question 4 different ways, run each through the model, and pick the answer most versions agree on
Run the same exact prompt 4 times and average the outputs
Ask the model to pick a random number between 1 and 4

exercises.explain: Option B is prompt ensembling: varying the WORDING of the prompt (not just resampling the same prompt) means each version has different quirks and blind spots. If most differently-phrased prompts converge on the same answer, that answer is far less likely to be an artifact of one particular phrasing. Option C only re-runs identical wording, so it doesn't protect against wording-specific bias. Option A relies entirely on one phrasing being perfect, which is fragile. Prompt ensembling is a simple, powerful way to make AI systems more robust to how questions happen to be asked.
